text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.

Review the attached incident documents and cybersecurity disclosure filings for SEC compliance gaps and prepare a comprehensive gap analysis memo.

Output: `gap-analysis-memo.docx`

**[PART I]{.underline}**

**[Item 1C. Cybersecurity]{.underline}**

Vantage Industrial Technologies, Inc. (\"Vantage,\" the \"Company,\" \"we,\" \"us,\" or \"our\") is a Delaware corporation headquartered at 4200 Commerce Park Drive, Suite 800, Columbus, Ohio 43219. The Company designs, manufactures, and distributes advanced industrial automation solutions, precision-engineered components, and related aftermarket parts and services through a network of 14 manufacturing facilities located across the United States, Germany, and Mexico, supported by approximately 6,800 employees worldwide. The Company recognizes the critical importance of cybersecurity in safeguarding its operations, intellectual property, proprietary manufacturing processes, customer data, and the integrity and availability of its information systems. Our manufacturing facilities and corporate operations depend on interconnected digital infrastructure, and any material disruption to these systems could adversely affect our ability to serve customers, maintain production schedules, and protect competitively sensitive information.

The following disclosure is made pursuant to Item 106 of Regulation S-K, as adopted by the Securities and Exchange Commission (the \"SEC\") in its final rules on \"Cybersecurity Risk Management, Strategy, Governance, and Incident Disclosure,\" effective September 5, 2023, and applicable to the Company beginning with this Annual Report on Form 10-K for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2023.

**Risk Management and Strategy**

**Cybersecurity Risk Management Program.** The Company maintains an enterprise-wide cybersecurity risk management program that is designed to assess, identify, and manage material risks arising from cybersecurity threats to its information technology systems, data, and operational infrastructure. This program is led by the Company\'s Chief Information Security Officer (\"CISO\"), Derek Fong, who joined the Company in March 2022. Prior to joining Vantage, Mr. Fong served in cybersecurity advisory and consulting roles with a nationally recognized cybersecurity consulting firm. The CISO reports to the Company\'s Chief Technology Officer (\"CTO\"), Lauren Hessler, who oversees the Company\'s broader technology strategy and operations.

The Company\'s cybersecurity risk management program encompasses a range of technical, administrative, and operational controls, including the following key elements:

> • **Network Monitoring and Security Operations.** The Company operates a 24/7 Security Operations Center (\"SOC\") that is responsible for continuous monitoring of the Company\'s network environment, including the detection, analysis, and initial triage of potential cybersecurity threats and anomalous activity across the Company\'s systems.
>
> • **Endpoint Detection and Response.** The Company has deployed endpoint detection and response capabilities across its approximately 2,100 networked endpoints, including workstations, laptops, servers, and mobile devices, to provide real-time visibility into endpoint activity and to enable rapid containment of identified threats.
>
> • **Vulnerability Scanning and Patch Management.** The Company conducts regular vulnerability scanning of its internal and external-facing systems, and maintains a patch management program designed to identify and remediate known software vulnerabilities in a timely manner based on assessed risk severity.
>
> • **Employee Cybersecurity Awareness Training.** The Company provides cybersecurity awareness training to its employees, including training on the identification and reporting of phishing attempts, social engineering tactics, and other common attack vectors. New employees receive cybersecurity awareness training as part of the onboarding process, and existing employees are required to complete annual refresher training.
>
> • **Access Management Controls.** The Company has implemented access management controls, including role-based access controls, privileged access management, and multi-factor authentication for access to critical information systems, remote access connections, and administrative accounts.
>
> • **IT/OT Network Segmentation.** The Company maintains an air-gap separation between its information technology (\"IT\") network and its operational technology (\"OT\") and supervisory control and data acquisition (\"SCADA\") systems that support the Company\'s manufacturing operations. This architectural control is designed to limit the potential for a compromise of the Company\'s IT network to propagate to the OT/SCADA environment, thereby reducing the risk that a cybersecurity incident originating in the IT environment could directly impact manufacturing operations, production continuity, or worker safety.

The Company\'s cybersecurity risk management program is designed to be adaptive in nature, and the Company periodically reviews and updates its security controls in response to changes in the threat landscape, the Company\'s operational environment, and applicable legal and regulatory requirements.

**Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan.** The Company maintains a Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (the \"CIRP\"), which establishes the Company\'s framework for responding to cybersecurity incidents in a coordinated, structured, and efficient manner. The CIRP was initially developed in June 2022 and was most recently reviewed in August 2023.

The CIRP establishes a structured incident response framework organized into five phases:

> \(1\) **Preparation** --- establishing and maintaining the readiness of the Company\'s incident response capabilities, including personnel, tools, and communications protocols;
>
> \(2\) **Detection and Analysis** --- identifying potential cybersecurity incidents through monitoring, alerting, and analysis, and determining the scope, severity, and nature of the incident;
>
> \(3\) **Containment** --- implementing measures to limit the spread and impact of a confirmed cybersecurity incident, including network isolation and access revocation as warranted;
>
> \(4\) **Eradication and Recovery** --- removing the threat actor and associated malicious artifacts from affected systems, restoring systems to normal operations, and validating the integrity of restored environments; and
>
> \(5\) **Post-Incident Review** --- conducting a structured review of the incident and the Company\'s response to identify lessons learned and opportunities for improvement.

Under the CIRP, the CISO serves as the Incident Commander and has authority to activate the Company\'s Incident Response Team (the \"IRT\") upon the identification of a cybersecurity incident meeting the applicable activation criteria. The IRT is a cross-functional team that includes representatives from information technology, information security, facilities management, and human resources, and is responsible for coordinating the Company\'s tactical response to cybersecurity incidents under the direction of the Incident Commander.

The CIRP includes a severity classification matrix with three tiers --- Tier 1, Tier 2, and Tier 3 --- which are used to prioritize response efforts and allocate resources based on assessed incident characteristics, including the nature and scope of the threat, the systems and data affected, and the potential for business disruption. The Company documents cybersecurity incidents in its incident management system to facilitate tracking, response coordination, and post-incident analysis.

**Third-Party Risk Management.** The Company utilizes third-party service providers in connection with certain aspects of its information technology infrastructure, including network maintenance, support services, cloud hosting, and data management. The Company recognizes that its reliance on third-party service providers introduces additional cybersecurity risks, and the Company seeks to manage these risks through a combination of vendor due diligence, contractual protections, and ongoing monitoring.

The Company maintains contractual requirements with key vendors that address data protection obligations, cybersecurity standards, breach notification requirements, and related matters. These contractual provisions are intended to align vendor cybersecurity practices with the Company\'s expectations and to provide the Company with appropriate rights and remedies in the event of a vendor-related cybersecurity incident.

The Company periodically engages third-party cybersecurity consultants to conduct independent assessments of the Company\'s cybersecurity posture, including penetration testing, vulnerability assessments, and reviews of the Company\'s security architecture and controls. The findings from these assessments are used by the CISO and the information security team to prioritize remediation activities and inform the ongoing development of the Company\'s cybersecurity risk management program.

In addition, the Company carries a cybersecurity liability insurance policy to help mitigate the financial impact of potential cybersecurity incidents, including costs associated with incident response, forensic investigation, data breach notification, credit monitoring, and potential legal liability. The Company periodically reviews the terms, conditions, and coverage limits of this policy in light of the evolving threat landscape and the Company\'s risk profile.

**Cybersecurity Risks and Impact.** The Company operates in the industrial automation sector, which faces particular cybersecurity risks given the sector\'s increasing reliance on networked manufacturing systems, the growing convergence of IT and OT environments, and the potential for cybersecurity incidents to disrupt production operations, compromise proprietary manufacturing processes, or result in the unauthorized disclosure of sensitive customer data and intellectual property. The Company\'s operations across multiple geographies further expose it to a range of region-specific threat actors and regulatory requirements.

As of the date of this filing, the Company has not experienced any cybersecurity incidents that have materially affected, or are reasonably likely to materially affect, the Company\'s business strategy, results of operations, or financial condition. However, the Company cannot provide assurance that future cybersecurity incidents will not occur or, if they do occur, that they will not materially affect the Company\'s business, financial condition, or results of operations. The Company faces evolving cybersecurity threats, including ransomware attacks, social engineering campaigns, supply chain compromise, advanced persistent threats, and insider threats. The sophistication and frequency of cyberattacks continue to increase, and threat actors are increasingly targeting industrial and manufacturing companies. Although the Company has implemented the cybersecurity risk management program and controls described herein, no cybersecurity program can eliminate all risk, and there can be no assurance that the Company\'s defenses will be sufficient to prevent or detect all cybersecurity incidents or that any incident that does occur will be contained or remediated in a timely manner. The Company\'s cybersecurity risk factors are described in greater detail in Item 1A (Risk Factors) of this Annual Report on Form 10-K.

**Governance.**

**Board of Directors Oversight.** The Board of Directors (the \"Board\"), acting primarily through its Audit Committee (the \"Audit Committee\"), oversees the Company\'s cybersecurity risk management program. The Audit Committee receives quarterly updates on the Company\'s cybersecurity posture. The Audit Committee is chaired by Dr. Helen Ostrowski, an independent director who also serves as a member of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. The Audit Committee consists of three independent directors, each of whom meets the independence requirements of the Nasdaq Stock Market listing standards and Rule 10A-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended.

The Audit Committee\'s charter establishes the committee\'s responsibilities with respect to the oversight of the Company\'s financial reporting, internal controls, compliance matters, and risk management. The Audit Committee meets at least quarterly and holds additional meetings as necessary to fulfill its oversight responsibilities. The Audit Committee regularly reports to the full Board on matters considered at its meetings and on any significant findings, recommendations, or concerns arising from its oversight activities.

In the event of a significant cybersecurity incident, the Company\'s processes provide for escalation to the Board of Directors and, as appropriate, the Audit Committee, to ensure that the Board is informed in a timely manner of developments that may affect the Company\'s operations, financial condition, or public disclosure obligations. The Board believes that the current governance structure provides appropriate oversight of cybersecurity risk within the broader context of the Company\'s enterprise risk management framework.

**Management\'s Role in Cybersecurity Risk Management.** The Company\'s cybersecurity risk management program is led by the CISO, Derek Fong. Mr. Fong has over a decade of experience in the cybersecurity field, including prior service as a senior consultant and engagement lead at a nationally recognized cybersecurity consulting firm, where he advised clients across the manufacturing, financial services, and technology sectors on cybersecurity strategy, incident response, and regulatory compliance. Mr. Fong holds the Certified Information Systems Security Professional (CISSP) and Certified Information Security Manager (CISM) designations, and he earned a Master of Science in Information Security from Carnegie Mellon University.

The CISO reports to the Company\'s Chief Technology Officer, Lauren Hessler, who in turn reports to the Chief Executive Officer, Marcus Ellsworth. In this capacity, the CISO is responsible for the day-to-day management of the Company\'s cybersecurity risk management program, including the development, implementation, and enforcement of cybersecurity policies, standards, and procedures. The CISO reports significant cybersecurity developments to the Chief Technology Officer, and the CTO incorporates cybersecurity matters into the technology updates provided to the Audit Committee as part of the CTO\'s quarterly reporting on the Company\'s technology strategy and operations. The CTO and CISO are jointly responsible for the development and implementation of the Company\'s cybersecurity policies, technical standards, and incident response procedures, and they work collaboratively to align the Company\'s cybersecurity investments with its technology roadmap and business priorities.

The Company\'s General Counsel provides legal guidance on cybersecurity matters as needed, including with respect to regulatory compliance, data privacy obligations, contractual requirements, and disclosure considerations. Management consults with the General Counsel on cybersecurity-related legal and regulatory developments that may affect the Company\'s operations or disclosure obligations.

The Company employs a team of approximately 22 information security professionals within the CISO\'s organization who are responsible for day-to-day cybersecurity operations, including threat monitoring through the SOC, vulnerability management, incident detection and response, security engineering, and identity and access management. This team includes personnel with specialized expertise in network security, cloud security, application security, and industrial control systems security. The Company also engages contract security personnel and managed security service providers to supplement the capabilities of its internal information security team as needed.

**Cybersecurity Strategy Integration.** Cybersecurity risk management is integrated into the Company\'s overall enterprise risk management (\"ERM\") framework. The Company conducts periodic risk assessments to identify and prioritize cybersecurity risks alongside other operational, financial, strategic, and compliance risks. The results of these assessments inform resource allocation decisions, cybersecurity program priorities, and the development of risk mitigation strategies.

The Company invests in cybersecurity technology, personnel, and training to strengthen its defenses against evolving threats. For the fiscal year ended December 31, 2023, the Company\'s cybersecurity-related expenditures were approximately \$12.4 million, representing an increase of approximately 18% compared to the prior fiscal year. These expenditures encompass costs associated with security technology platforms, SOC operations, personnel compensation and benefits, training and professional development, third-party assessments and consulting engagements, and cybersecurity insurance premiums.

The Company periodically evaluates its cybersecurity program against industry frameworks, including the National Institute of Standards and Technology (\"NIST\") Cybersecurity Framework (CSF), to benchmark its practices, identify areas for improvement, and promote alignment with recognized cybersecurity standards. The Company uses the insights gained from these evaluations, together with findings from internal assessments and third-party reviews, to inform the continued evolution and maturation of its cybersecurity risk management program.

**Cautionary Statement Regarding Forward-Looking Statements**

Certain statements in this Item 1C contain forward-looking statements within the meaning of Section 27A of the Securities Act of 1933, as amended, and Section 21E of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended. These forward-looking statements include, but are not limited to, statements regarding the Company\'s cybersecurity risk management program, its strategies for managing cybersecurity risks, and the expected effectiveness of its cybersecurity controls and incident response procedures. Forward-looking statements are based on the Company\'s current expectations and assumptions regarding future events and are subject to risks, uncertainties, and other factors that could cause actual results to differ materially from those described in the forward-looking statements. These risks and uncertainties include, among others, the risks described in Item 1A (Risk Factors) of this Annual Report on Form 10-K. The Company undertakes no obligation to update or revise any forward-looking statements, whether as a result of new information, future events, or otherwise, except as required by applicable law.

This excerpt constitutes Item 1C of Part I of the Annual Report on Form 10-K for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2023, as filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission on February 28, 2024. This excerpt should be read in conjunction with the full Annual Report, including Item 1A (Risk Factors), Item 7 (Management\'s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations), and the consolidated financial statements and notes thereto included elsewhere in this Form 10-K.

**[CHARTER OF THE AUDIT COMMITTEE]{.underline}**

**[OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS]{.underline}**

**[OF VANTAGE INDUSTRIAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC.]{.underline}**

*As Last Amended and Restated on March 15, 2021*

**[I. Purpose]{.underline}**

The Audit Committee (the \"Committee\") of the Board of Directors (the \"Board\") of Vantage Industrial Technologies, Inc., a Delaware corporation (the \"Company\"), whose common stock is listed on the NASDAQ Global Select Market under the ticker symbol \"VTIQ,\" is established by the Board to assist the Board in fulfilling its oversight responsibilities. The Committee\'s primary purpose is to oversee and monitor: (1) the integrity of the Company\'s financial statements and financial reporting processes; (2) the Company\'s compliance with legal and regulatory requirements related to financial reporting, including applicable securities laws and regulations; (3) the qualifications, independence, and performance of the Company\'s independent registered public accounting firm (the \"independent auditor\"); (4) the performance of the Company\'s internal audit function; and (5) such other matters as may be specifically delegated to the Committee by the Board from time to time.

In carrying out its oversight responsibilities, the Committee shall endeavor to maintain free and open communication among its members, the independent auditor, the internal audit function, and the management of the Company. The Committee shall be empowered to act on behalf of the Board with respect to any matter within the scope of this Charter to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, the Company\'s Certificate of Incorporation, and the Company\'s Amended and Restated Bylaws.

**[II. Composition and Membership]{.underline}**

The Committee shall consist of no fewer than three (3) members of the Board. Each member of the Committee shall satisfy the independence requirements established by NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(a)(2) and Rule 10A-3 promulgated under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the \"Exchange Act\"). No member of the Committee may have participated in the preparation of the financial statements of the Company or any current subsidiary of the Company at any time during the preceding three (3) years.

Each member of the Committee shall be financially literate, as determined by the Board in the exercise of its business judgment, or shall become financially literate within a reasonable period of time after his or her appointment to the Committee. At least one member of the Committee shall qualify as an \"audit committee financial expert\" as that term is defined in Item 407(d)(5) of Regulation S-K promulgated by the Securities and Exchange Commission (the \"SEC\"). The designation of a member as an audit committee financial expert shall not impose any duties, obligations, or liabilities on such member that are greater than those imposed on other Committee members or on members of the Board generally.

Members of the Committee shall be appointed annually by the Board, upon the recommendation of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee, and shall serve until their successors are duly appointed and qualified, or until their earlier resignation, removal, or death. The Board may remove any member of the Committee at any time, with or without cause. The Board shall designate one member of the Committee to serve as Chair. In the absence of the Chair at any meeting, the Committee may elect a temporary presiding member from among its members. Vacancies on the Committee shall be filled by the Board upon the recommendation of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee.

**[III. Meetings and Procedures]{.underline}**

The Committee shall meet at least four (4) times per fiscal year and more frequently as circumstances may require. Meetings shall typically be held on a quarterly basis in conjunction with the Company\'s quarterly financial reporting cycle to coincide with the review and filing of the Company\'s periodic reports with the SEC. Special meetings of the Committee may be called by the Chair, by any two members of the Committee, or by the Board.

The Chair shall preside at each meeting and, in consultation with the Company\'s Chief Financial Officer (the \"CFO\"), the Company\'s General Counsel, and the independent auditor, shall establish the agenda for each meeting. Each Committee member may suggest the inclusion of items on the agenda, and the Committee may consider matters not on the formal agenda at any meeting. Meeting materials shall be distributed to Committee members in advance of each meeting to the extent practicable.

A majority of the members of the Committee shall constitute a quorum for the transaction of business. The Committee shall act on the affirmative vote of a majority of the members present at a meeting at which a quorum is present. The Committee may also act by unanimous written consent in lieu of a meeting, to the extent permitted by applicable law and the Company\'s Amended and Restated Bylaws.

The Committee may invite members of management, the independent auditor, internal auditors, and such outside advisors as the Committee deems appropriate to attend meetings and to provide relevant information. The Committee shall meet periodically in executive session without members of management present, and shall meet separately with representatives of the independent auditor and with the head of the internal audit function. The Committee shall maintain written minutes of its meetings, which shall be filed with the Corporate Secretary and made available to the full Board. The Committee shall report regularly to the Board on its activities, findings, and recommendations. The Corporate Secretary shall assist the Committee with the scheduling of meetings and the maintenance of Committee records.

**[IV. Authority and Resources]{.underline}**

The Committee shall have the sole authority to appoint, compensate, retain, oversee, evaluate, and, where appropriate, terminate the Company\'s independent registered public accounting firm. The independent auditor shall report directly to the Committee. As of the date of this Charter, the Company\'s independent auditor is Greystone & Associates LLP.

The Committee shall have the authority, without seeking prior approval of the Board, to retain and obtain the advice and assistance of independent legal counsel, accounting advisors, and such other advisors as the Committee determines to be necessary or appropriate in the performance of its duties. The Company shall provide the Committee with appropriate funding, as determined by the Committee in its capacity as a committee of the Board, for: (a) payment of compensation to the independent auditor engaged for the purpose of preparing or issuing an audit report or performing other audit, review, or attest services for the Company; (b) payment of compensation to any advisors employed or retained by the Committee; and (c) ordinary administrative expenses of the Committee that are necessary or appropriate in carrying out its duties.

The Committee shall have direct and unrestricted access to the Company\'s internal audit function, the independent auditor, and such officers and employees of the Company and its subsidiaries as the Committee deems necessary to carry out its duties. Officers and employees of the Company shall be directed to cooperate fully with the Committee\'s requests for information and assistance.

The Committee may form and delegate authority to subcommittees consisting of one or more of its members, including the authority to grant pre-approvals of audit and permissible non-audit services, provided that each such subcommittee shall consist of at least two (2) independent members of the Committee, and provided further that the decisions of any subcommittee shall be presented to the full Committee at its next regularly scheduled meeting.

**[V. Duties and Responsibilities]{.underline}**

The following duties and responsibilities are set forth as a guide for the Committee in fulfilling its oversight function. The Committee may supplement these duties as appropriate and may establish policies and procedures to carry out these duties consistent with the Company\'s Amended and Restated Bylaws, applicable law, and the rules and regulations of the SEC and NASDAQ. The Committee shall have the authority to carry out each of the following responsibilities:

**A. Financial Reporting Oversight**

1\. Review and discuss with management and the independent auditor the Company\'s annual audited financial statements, including the related notes thereto, prior to the filing of the Company\'s Annual Report on Form 10-K with the SEC. Such review shall include a discussion of the quality, not just the acceptability, of the Company\'s accounting principles, the reasonableness of significant judgments, and the clarity of disclosures in the financial statements.

2\. Review and discuss with management and the independent auditor the Company\'s quarterly unaudited financial statements, including the related notes thereto, prior to the filing of the Company\'s Quarterly Report on Form 10-Q with the SEC. This review shall encompass the results of the independent auditor\'s review of the quarterly financial statements conducted pursuant to Statement on Auditing Standards No. 100.

3\. Review and discuss with management and the independent auditor the Company\'s disclosures contained in \"Management\'s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations\" included in the Company\'s periodic reports filed with the SEC.

4\. Review earnings press releases and financial information and earnings guidance provided to analysts and rating agencies, including the types and presentation of information to be included in such communications, paying particular attention to the use of any \"pro forma\" or \"adjusted\" non-GAAP financial information.

5\. Discuss with management and the independent auditor significant financial reporting issues and judgments made in connection with the preparation of the Company\'s financial statements, including critical accounting estimates, changes in the Company\'s selection or application of accounting principles, and the effects of regulatory and accounting initiatives, as well as any off-balance sheet structures, on the Company\'s financial statements.

6\. Review and discuss with management and the independent auditor the adequacy and effectiveness of the Company\'s disclosure controls and procedures and internal controls over financial reporting, including any significant deficiencies or material weaknesses in the design or operation of such controls identified by the independent auditor or the internal audit function, and any fraud, whether or not material, that involves management or other employees who have a significant role in the Company\'s internal controls.

7\. Review the report of management and the attestation report of the independent auditor on the Company\'s internal control over financial reporting, as required by Section 404 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, prior to the filing of the Company\'s Annual Report on Form 10-K.

8\. Discuss with the independent auditor the matters required to be communicated by the applicable standards of the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (the \"PCAOB\"), including, but not limited to, critical audit matters, significant audit findings, and any difficulties encountered during the course of the audit.

**B. Independent Auditor Oversight**

1\. Appoint, compensate, retain, and oversee the work of the independent registered public accounting firm (currently Greystone & Associates LLP), which shall report directly to the Committee. The Committee shall have the sole authority to approve the engagement and fee arrangements with the independent auditor.

2\. Pre-approve all audit services and permissible non-audit services to be provided by the independent auditor, subject to the de minimis exceptions for non-audit services described in Section 10A(i)(1)(B) of the Exchange Act that are approved by the Committee prior to the completion of the audit. The Committee may establish pre-approval policies and procedures consistent with applicable SEC rules for the engagement of the independent auditor to provide certain specified services.

3\. Evaluate the qualifications, performance, and independence of the independent auditor at least annually, taking into account the opinions of management and the internal auditor. Such evaluation shall include: (a) a review of the independent auditor\'s internal quality-control procedures; (b) a review of any material issues raised by the most recent internal quality-control review or peer review of the auditor, or by any inquiry or investigation by governmental or professional authorities within the preceding five (5) years, respecting one or more independent audits carried out by the firm; and (c) an assessment of the impact of any such issues on the auditor\'s qualifications and ability to serve the Company.

4\. Obtain and review at least annually a formal written statement from the independent auditor delineating all relationships between the auditor and the Company, consistent with the applicable requirements of PCAOB Ethics and Independence Rule 3526, *Communication with Audit Committees Concerning Independence*. The Committee shall actively engage in a dialogue with the independent auditor with respect to any disclosed relationships or services that may impact the objectivity and independence of the auditor and shall take appropriate action in response to the independent auditor\'s report to satisfy itself of the auditor\'s independence.

5\. Assure the regular rotation of the lead audit partner and concurring review partner of the independent auditor as required by applicable law, SEC rules, and PCAOB standards, and consider whether there should be periodic rotation of the accounting firm itself.

6\. Review and discuss the scope and results of the annual audit with the independent auditor, including any audit problems or difficulties encountered during the course of the audit work and management\'s response, as well as any restrictions on the scope of the auditor\'s activities or access to requested information.

7\. Establish clear policies regarding the hiring by the Company of employees or former employees of the independent auditor, consistent with applicable SEC rules and listing standards.

**C. Internal Audit Oversight**

1\. Review and approve the annual internal audit plan, including the scope, staffing, organizational structure, and budget of the Company\'s internal audit function, and any significant changes thereto. The Committee shall ensure that the internal audit function has adequate resources and appropriate authority to fulfill its responsibilities.

2\. Review significant reports to management prepared by the internal audit function and management\'s responses and follow-up actions with respect to the findings and recommendations contained therein.

3\. Review and concur in the appointment, replacement, reassignment, or dismissal of the head of the Company\'s internal audit function (the \"Chief Audit Executive\"). The Chief Audit Executive shall report functionally to the Committee and administratively to the Chief Financial Officer.

4\. Discuss with the Chief Audit Executive and management the internal audit function\'s responsibilities, budget, staffing, and any issues that the Chief Audit Executive believes warrant Committee attention.

**D. Compliance and Risk Management**

1\. Discuss with management the Company\'s major financial risk exposures and the steps management has taken to monitor and control such exposures, including the Company\'s risk assessment and risk management policies and guidelines as they relate to the Company\'s financial condition, financial risk, and financial reporting. The Committee shall review with management the Company\'s guidelines and policies governing the process by which financial risk assessment and financial risk management are undertaken.

2\. Review with the General Counsel any legal matters, including pending or threatened litigation, government investigations, or regulatory proceedings, that could have a significant impact on the Company\'s financial statements or financial condition, and any material reports or inquiries received from governmental or regulatory agencies relating to financial reporting or accounting matters.

3\. Review with management the Company\'s compliance with applicable laws and regulations as they relate to financial reporting and securities law compliance, including the requirements of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 and the rules and regulations promulgated thereunder.

4\. Establish and oversee procedures for: (a) the receipt, retention, and treatment of complaints received by the Company regarding accounting, internal accounting controls, or auditing matters; and (b) the confidential, anonymous submission by employees of the Company of concerns regarding questionable accounting or auditing matters (the \"Whistleblower Procedures\"). The Committee shall periodically review the effectiveness of the Whistleblower Procedures and any complaints or concerns received thereunder.

5\. Review the Company\'s Code of Ethics for Senior Financial Officers and any waivers of, or amendments to, such Code, and recommend any changes thereto to the Board.

6\. Review with management any correspondence with regulators or governmental agencies, including the SEC and NASDAQ, and any published reports that raise material issues regarding the Company\'s financial statements or accounting policies.

**E. Related Party Transactions**

1\. Review and approve or ratify all related party transactions required to be disclosed pursuant to Item 404 of Regulation S-K, in accordance with the Company\'s Related Party Transaction Policy. The Committee shall review and assess the adequacy of such Policy on a periodic basis and recommend any changes thereto to the Board.

**F. Other Responsibilities**

1\. Prepare the report of the Committee required by the rules and regulations of the SEC for inclusion in the Company\'s annual proxy statement (the \"Audit Committee Report\").

2\. Conduct an annual self-assessment of the Committee\'s performance and the adequacy of this Charter, including a review of the Committee\'s compliance with this Charter, and report the results of such assessment to the Board.

3\. Review and reassess the adequacy of this Charter at least annually and recommend any proposed changes to the Board for approval.

4\. Perform such other duties and responsibilities as may be assigned to the Committee by the Board from time to time, or as may be required by applicable law, the rules and regulations of the SEC, or NASDAQ listing standards.

**[VI. Limitations of the Committee\'s Role]{.underline}**

The Committee\'s function is one of oversight. The management of the Company is responsible for the preparation, presentation, and integrity of the Company\'s financial statements and for the appropriateness of the accounting and financial reporting principles and policies and internal controls and procedures designed to assure compliance with applicable accounting standards, laws, and regulations. The Company\'s independent auditor is responsible for planning and carrying out a proper audit of the Company\'s annual financial statements in accordance with the standards of the PCAOB and for reviewing the Company\'s quarterly financial statements.

The Committee recognizes that the Company\'s management and the independent auditor possess greater resources, time, and detailed knowledge and information regarding the Company\'s accounting, auditing, and financial reporting practices than do the members of the Committee. Accordingly, the Committee\'s oversight role does not provide an independent basis for certifying or assuring that management has maintained appropriate accounting and financial reporting principles or policies, or that appropriate internal controls and procedures have been designed and implemented, that the Company\'s financial statements are complete and accurate and fairly present the Company\'s financial condition and results of operations, or that the Company\'s audit has been conducted in accordance with generally accepted auditing standards and the standards of the PCAOB.

The responsibilities and duties of the Committee as set forth in this Charter are not intended to create, and shall not be construed as creating, any duty, obligation, or liability of the Committee, any member thereof, or the Board that is greater than the duties, obligations, or liabilities otherwise imposed on the Committee, its members, or the Board under applicable federal or state law. Nothing in this Charter shall be construed to alter or diminish the responsibilities or liabilities of the Company\'s management or independent auditor.

**[VII. Amendment History and Effective Date]{.underline}**

This Charter was originally adopted by the Board of Directors of the Company on June 12, 2017. This Charter has been amended as set forth below:

> • **September 20, 2018** --- Amended to conform to updated NASDAQ listing standards regarding audit committee independence requirements and to incorporate revisions to the Committee\'s independent auditor oversight procedures.
>
> • **March 15, 2021** --- Amended to update references to current PCAOB standards, to add provisions regarding the oversight and review of related party transactions pursuant to Item 404 of Regulation S-K, and to make certain other conforming and administrative changes.

This Charter shall remain in effect unless and until further amended, restated, or superseded by the Board of Directors of the Company.

**APPROVAL**

As last amended and approved by the Board of Directors of Vantage Industrial Technologies, Inc. on March 15, 2021.

**[Section 1 --- Purpose and Scope]{.underline}**

**[1.1 Purpose]{.underline}**

This Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (the \"Plan\") establishes the procedures and organizational framework for identifying, containing, eradicating, and recovering from cybersecurity incidents affecting the information technology systems and data assets of Vantage Industrial Technologies, Inc. (the \"Company\"). The Plan provides a structured methodology to ensure that cybersecurity events are addressed in a timely, organized, and effective manner by trained personnel using pre-defined procedures and communication channels.

This Plan is designed to minimize disruption to business operations, protect company and customer data, and ensure timely restoration of affected systems. By establishing clear roles, responsibilities, escalation paths, and response procedures, this Plan aims to reduce the potential impact of cybersecurity incidents on the Company\'s technology infrastructure and to promote a culture of security awareness and operational resilience across the organization. The procedures set forth herein are informed by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) and NIST Special Publication 800-61, Revision 2 (*Computer Security Incident Handling Guide*), and are intended to reflect industry best practices for incident response within an industrial manufacturing environment.

**[1.2 Scope]{.underline}**

This Plan applies to all cybersecurity incidents affecting information technology (IT) systems, networks, applications, and data owned, operated, or managed by Vantage Industrial Technologies, Inc., including systems hosted by third-party service providers on behalf of the Company. The scope of this Plan encompasses the Company\'s entire IT footprint, including but not limited to:

> • Corporate headquarters located at 4200 Commerce Park Drive, Suite 800, Columbus, Ohio 43219;
>
> • All fourteen (14) manufacturing facilities operated by the Company across the United States, Germany, and Mexico;
>
> • Remote access infrastructure, including virtual private network (VPN) gateways and associated authentication systems;
>
> • Cloud-hosted applications and data repositories;
>
> • Enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems and associated modules (finance, procurement, order management);
>
> • Email systems, collaboration platforms, and internal communication tools; and
>
> • Approximately 2,100 networked endpoints across the enterprise, including workstations, servers, laptops, and mobile devices enrolled in the Company\'s endpoint management platform.

This Plan addresses incidents affecting the Company\'s Information Technology (IT) environment. Operational Technology (OT) and SCADA systems are governed by separate operational safety procedures and are outside the scope of this Plan. The Company maintains the IT and OT networks as air-gapped environments to reduce the risk of cross-domain compromise. Any incident that is suspected of affecting OT or SCADA systems should be immediately reported to the Director of Manufacturing Operations in addition to the Incident Commander, and the applicable OT safety procedures shall take precedence in such scenarios.

**[1.3 Applicability]{.underline}**

This Plan applies to all employees, contractors, temporary workers, and third-party vendors who have been granted access to Vantage Industrial Technologies, Inc.\'s IT systems, networks, or data. As of the effective date of this Plan, the Company employs approximately 6,800 individuals across its corporate and manufacturing operations. In addition, the Company maintains VPN-credentialed access for authorized third-party maintenance contractors who provide remote and on-site technical support for various IT and manufacturing support systems. All persons within the scope of this Plan are expected to comply with the Company\'s information security policies and to promptly report any suspected cybersecurity incidents to the Security Operations Center (SOC) or their immediate supervisor.

**[1.4 Document Governance]{.underline}**

This Plan shall be reviewed annually by the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) and updated as necessary to reflect changes in the Company\'s technology environment, threat landscape, organizational structure, or operational requirements. Following any substantive revision, the Plan shall be submitted to the Chief Technology Officer (CTO) for re-approval prior to re-issuance.

The most recent substantive review was conducted in June 2022. An administrative review was completed on August 14, 2023, updating vendor contact information in Appendix C. No changes were made to the Plan\'s procedures, governance structure, classification framework, or communication protocols during the August 2023 review.

No tabletop exercises or simulation-based tests of this Plan have been conducted. The CISO shall develop an exercise schedule and ensure that the Plan is tested through periodic simulations. The version history of this Plan is maintained at the end of this document.

All questions regarding this Plan should be directed to the CISO. The CISO shall maintain the master copy of this Plan and is responsible for distributing updated versions to all IRT members and other designated personnel.

**[Section 2 --- Governance and Organizational Authority]{.underline}**

**[2.1 Plan Ownership]{.underline}**

The Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), currently Derek Fong, is the owner of this Plan and is responsible for its maintenance, periodic review, updates, and activation. The CISO reports to the Chief Technology Officer (CTO), currently Lauren Hessler, within the Company\'s organizational structure. The CISO shall coordinate with the Information Technology, Information Security, Facilities, and Human Resources departments in executing this Plan. The CISO shall ensure that all IRT members have access to the current version of this Plan and are familiar with their roles and responsibilities as described herein.

**[2.2 Incident Commander]{.underline}**

The CISO serves as the \"Incident Commander\" for all cybersecurity incidents governed by this Plan. The Incident Commander is the central authority responsible for directing the Company\'s response to any cybersecurity incident from initial detection through final closure. The Incident Commander position is critical to maintaining a unified, coordinated response effort and ensuring that all response activities are properly documented and executed in accordance with established procedures.

The Incident Commander has the authority to:

> \(a\) Activate the Incident Response Team (IRT) and convene IRT members for coordinated response activities;
>
> \(b\) Declare and assign incident severity levels in accordance with the classification criteria set forth in Section 3.2 of this Plan;
>
> \(c\) Authorize containment actions, including but not limited to system isolation, network segmentation, credential revocation, and emergency firewall rule implementation;
>
> \(d\) Engage pre-approved third-party forensic and remediation vendors under existing retainer agreements, as listed in Appendix C of this Plan;
>
> \(e\) Authorize emergency IT expenditures up to a cumulative total of \$250,000 per incident without requiring additional management approval, provided that such expenditures are documented in the ServiceNow incident record and reported to the CTO within forty-eight (48) hours; and
>
> \(f\) Determine when the incident has been fully resolved and authorize closure of the incident record in ServiceNow.

The Incident Commander shall exercise sole authority over incident classification, response coordination, and closure determination. In the event that the CISO is unavailable, the Director of IT Infrastructure shall serve as the alternate Incident Commander until the CISO is available to resume command. The Incident Commander may delegate specific tasks to IRT members but retains overall accountability for the response effort.
